questions.prompt: Why is `200 OK` with `{"status": "error"}` body a bad pattern?

choices.text: Breaks HTTP semantics that infrastructure depends on. 200 means success; the entire stack — clients, proxies, CDNs, error trackers, monitoring tools, retry libraries — assumes 200 = success. With 200-but-error, every client needs custom unwrapping (`if (response.status === 200 && body.status === 'error')`); monitoring shows 100% success rate when actually 30% of requests fail; retry libraries don't retry failed requests; Sentry / DataDog don't alert on errors. Real status codes (4xx for client errors, 5xx for server errors) plug into all of this for free: monitoring auto-tracks error rates, retries happen automatically for 5xx, clients can use standard error handling. The 'easier client code' justification for 200-with-error backfires every time. Use 422 for validation errors, 404 for missing resources, 409 for conflicts, 401/403 for auth, 500/503 for server issues. The HTTP semantic system was designed for this; using it gives you observability and correct retry behavior automatically.

questions.explanation: 200 = success per HTTP. Infra (monitoring, retries, error trackers) all assume it. Use real codes.

questions.prompt: User submits a form with an invalid email. What status code?

choices.text: 422 Unprocessable Entity. The distinction: 400 = malformed request (couldn't even parse the JSON, missing required header, wrong Content-Type); 422 = request is well-formed and parseable but semantically invalid (email format wrong, age below minimum, required field absent). 422 is more specific and lets clients distinguish 'I sent garbage' (400 — usually a code bug) from 'I sent a valid request that failed validation' (422 — show the user a friendly form error). Pair with a structured body listing the failing fields: `{"error": "validation_failed", "fields": {"email": "must be valid email"}}`. Many APIs use 400 for validation errors out of habit; 422 is more precise and supports better client UX. Some teams use 400 for both because '422 feels obscure'; either works if consistent, but 422 is the spec-aligned choice for validation failures.

questions.explanation: 422 — well-formed request, validation failed. 400 is for unparseable/malformed; 422 is for validation.
